---
title: "Food-security impact"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
import sys, pathlib
import pandas as pd

REPO_ROOT = None
for _cand in (pathlib.Path.cwd(), *pathlib.Path.cwd().parents):
    if (_cand / "CLAUDE.md").exists() and (_cand / "viz").is_dir():
        REPO_ROOT = _cand
        sys.path.insert(0, str(_cand))
        break
assert REPO_ROOT is not None, "could not locate repo root (CLAUDE.md + viz/)"
OUT = REPO_ROOT / "outputs"

nat = pd.read_csv(OUT / "food_security" / "impact_national.csv")
impact = pd.read_csv(OUT / "food_security" / "impact_by_aoi.csv")
tot = nat.loc[nat["aoi_id"] == "STUDY_TOTAL"].iloc[0]
n_govs = (nat["aoi_id"] != "STUDY_TOTAL").sum()
```

The primary question: **how much of the 2026 cereal recovery did the floods and fires
reverse, and where?** Damaged cropland hectares become an estimated production loss,
then an indicative food-security pressure, referenced against the 2025 drought
baseline.

::: {.key-findings}
### The answer

```{python}
#| label: headline
#| echo: false
from IPython.display import Markdown
Markdown(f"""
- **~{tot.production_loss_t_headline:,.0f} t** of cereal production lost across
  **{n_govs} governorates** (3 flood AOIs + the national fire set), a lower bound.
  Range **{tot.production_loss_t_low:,.0f}–{tot.production_loss_t_season_ref:,.0f} t**
  from cropland-definition and temporal-aggregation sensitivity.
- That is **{tot.loss_pct_of_baseline_headline:.2f}%** of the combined 2025 baseline
  ({tot.baseline_production_2025_t:,.0f} t), about 2.16% of the national ~1.2 Mt floor.
- Indicative food-security pressure (study total): **{tot.food_security_pressure_indicative}**,
  *not* an IPC phase (see method note).
- The damage is **concentrated**: the two Euphrates flood AOIs (Raqqa, Deir ez-Zor)
  each lose ~7–8% of their own baseline; the other nine governorates lose under 0.4%
  each.
""")
```
:::

::: {.callout-important}
**First-half-2026 snapshot — figures are lower bounds.** Windows cover floods Mar–Jun
and fires May 1 – Jun 12. The harvest and fire season peaks in summer (Jun–Aug), so
the heaviest crop-fire months are unobserved. Re-run after the season closes for the
concluded result; field and expert verification is preferred over remote-sensing
self-consistency ([DEC-039](glossary.qmd#dec-039)).
:::

## Where the loss falls

```{python}
#| label: fig-loss-map
#| echo: false
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-dpi: 200
#| fig-cap: "Cereal-production loss as a share of each governorate's 2025 baseline. The Euphrates corridor carries the damage; Latakia and Damascus City are verified-excluded (grey). First-half-2026 lower bound."
#| fig-alt: "Choropleth map of Syria shaded by 2026 cropland-production loss as a percent of the 2025 baseline. Raqqa and Deir ez-Zor are darkest at about 7.6 to 7.8 percent, Hasakah mid-toned at 2.8 percent, all others pale under 1.5 percent, Latakia and Damascus City grey."
from viz import choropleth, caveat_footer, CAVEATS
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

govs = nat[nat.aoi_id != "STUDY_TOTAL"]
vals = dict(zip(govs.aoi_id, govs.loss_pct_of_baseline_headline))
fig = choropleth(vals, title="2026 cropland-production loss (% of 2025 baseline)",
                 cbar_label="loss, % of 2025 baseline", value_fmt="{:.1f}%")
caveat_footer(fig, CAVEATS["case_study_2026h1"])
plt.show()
```

## The whiplash

The study's spine is a three-state sequence, **drought → tentative recovery →
shock**, and the question is how much of the recovery the 2026 shock took back.

| State | Cereal production | Note |
|---|---:|---|
| 2025 — record drought (baseline) | ~1.20 Mt | ~60%+ below average; worst in ~60 years (FAO/GIEWS) |
| 2026 — tentative recovery (projected) | ~2.30 Mt | more even rainfall; sowing targets not fully met |
| 2026 — observed first-half shock | see below | floods + fires, validated damage only |

The recovery figure is an external projection (context). The shock is measured here
from **human-validated damage records only**. The ~25.9 kt loss is about **2.4% of
the ~1.1 Mt recovery increment** the 2026 harvest was projected to add back: a real
dent in the rebound, not a reversal of it.

## Production loss by governorate

```{python}
#| label: fig-loss-by-aoi
#| fig-cap: "Estimated 2026 cereal-production loss (headline t) by governorate and phenomenon. Floods on the Euphrates dominate; the national fire set adds a broad but individually small contribution. First-half-2026 lower bound."
#| fig-alt: "Grouped bar chart of estimated 2026 cereal-production loss in tonnes by governorate, split into flood (blue) and fire (red). Raqqa, Hasakah and Deir ez-Zor have by far the largest flood losses (roughly 10,000, 8,600 and 5,000 tonnes); all fire bars are under about 920 tonnes."
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from viz import apply_theme, caveat_footer, CAVEATS, PHENOMENON_COLORS, bar_value_labels

pal = {k.value: v for k, v in PHENOMENON_COLORS.items()}
apply_theme()
order = (impact.groupby("aoi_id")["production_loss_t_headline"].sum()
               .sort_values(ascending=False).index.tolist())
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
sns.barplot(
    impact, x="aoi_id", y="production_loss_t_headline", hue="phenomenon",
    hue_order=["flood", "fire"], order=order, palette=pal, ax=ax,
)
bar_value_labels(ax, fmt="{:,.0f}", threshold=200)
ax.set_xlabel("Governorate")
ax.set_ylabel("Estimated production loss — headline (t)")
ax.set_title("2026 cereal-production loss by governorate and phenomenon")
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=35)
for lbl in ax.get_xticklabels():
    lbl.set_ha("right")
ax.legend(title="", loc="upper right", frameon=True)
caveat_footer(fig, CAVEATS["case_study_2026h1"])
plt.show()
```

[↓ Data behind this figure (impact_by_aoi.csv)](https://github.com/Muhanad-husn/flood-fire/blob/main/outputs/food_security/impact_by_aoi.csv){.data-download}

## Per-governorate detail

Loss as a share of each governorate's own 2025 baseline shows where the shock bites
hardest in *relative* terms. The three Euphrates/Khabur AOIs carry essentially all of
it; the nine fire-only governorates are individually marginal, so they are summarised
into one row below (the full 12-row table is one click away).

```{python}
#| label: tbl-per-aoi
#| echo: false
g = nat[nat["aoi_id"] != "STUDY_TOTAL"].copy()
prominent = g[g["food_security_pressure_indicative"] != "marginal"].copy()
marginal = g[g["food_security_pressure_indicative"] == "marginal"].copy()

prominent = prominent.sort_values("production_loss_t_headline", ascending=False)
summary = pd.DataFrame([{
    "aoi_id": f"{len(marginal)} other governorates (each <0.4%)",
    "baseline_production_2025_t": marginal["baseline_production_2025_t"].sum(),
    "production_loss_t_headline": marginal["production_loss_t_headline"].sum(),
    "production_loss_t_low": marginal["production_loss_t_low"].sum(),
    "production_loss_t_season_ref": marginal["production_loss_t_season_ref"].sum(),
    "loss_pct_of_baseline_headline": (
        marginal["production_loss_t_headline"].sum()
        / marginal["baseline_production_2025_t"].sum() * 100),
    "food_security_pressure_indicative": "marginal (combined)",
}])
cols = ["aoi_id", "baseline_production_2025_t", "production_loss_t_headline",
        "production_loss_t_low", "production_loss_t_season_ref",
        "loss_pct_of_baseline_headline", "food_security_pressure_indicative"]
view = pd.concat([prominent[cols], summary[cols]], ignore_index=True).rename(columns={
    "aoi_id": "governorate",
    "baseline_production_2025_t": "baseline 2025 (t)",
    "production_loss_t_headline": "loss headline (t)",
    "production_loss_t_low": "loss low (t)",
    "production_loss_t_season_ref": "loss season-ref (t)",
    "loss_pct_of_baseline_headline": "loss % of baseline",
    "food_security_pressure_indicative": "indicative pressure",
})
(view.style.hide(axis="index")
     .format({"baseline 2025 (t)": "{:,.0f}", "loss headline (t)": "{:,.1f}",
              "loss low (t)": "{:,.1f}", "loss season-ref (t)": "{:,.1f}",
              "loss % of baseline": "{:.2f}"})
     .bar(subset=["loss % of baseline"], color="#9ecae1",
          vmin=0, vmax=float(g["loss_pct_of_baseline_headline"].max())))
```

[↓ Full 12-governorate table (impact_national.csv)](https://github.com/Muhanad-husn/flood-fire/blob/main/outputs/food_security/impact_national.csv){.data-download}

## Robustness — the headline without the RQ1-flagged hectares

[RQ1](rq1.qmd) flags Hasakah's June flood hectares as a likely radar harvest artifact
and excludes them from attribution. To keep the food-security number and the RQ1
attribution on the *same* record set, here is the study total recomputed with those
flagged dates removed (Hasakah's flood headline falls back to its attributed March
peak).

```{python}
#| label: excl-flagged
#| echo: false
events = pd.read_csv(OUT / "floods" / "rq1_attribution" / "event_mechanism.csv")
YIELD = float(impact["baseline_yield_t_ha"].iloc[0])  # DEC-019 drought-floor yield

attributed = events[events["mechanism"] != "unexplained"]
peak_attr = attributed.groupby("aoi_id")["flooded_cropland_ha_union"].max()

flood = impact[impact["phenomenon"] == "flood"].set_index("aoi_id")
removed_t = 0.0
for aoi, row in flood.iterrows():
    new_ha = peak_attr.get(aoi, row["damaged_ha_headline_union"])
    removed_t += row["production_loss_t_headline"] - new_ha * YIELD

headline = tot.production_loss_t_headline
excl = headline - removed_t
base = tot.baseline_production_2025_t
Markdown(f"""
| | Production loss | % of 2025 floor |
|---|---:|---:|
| **Headline (all validated records)** | ~{headline:,.0f} t | {headline/base*100:.2f}% |
| **Excluding RQ1-flagged Hasakah-June hectares** | ~{excl:,.0f} t | {excl/base*100:.2f}% |
| *Difference* | *−{removed_t:,.0f} t* | *−{removed_t/headline*100:.1f}%* |
""")
```

The headline is **robust to the flag**: removing the flagged hectares moves it only
about **5%**. That is because the layer already counts each AOI's **peak single flood
date**, not the sum of dates ([DEC-033](glossary.qmd#dec-033)), so the flagged June
dates never compounded, and the total is dominated by the well-supported Euphrates
records. We publish both figures rather than choosing one.

## Method, and what the numbers are not

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Method & provenance (expand)

- **Validated-only gate.** Only human-validated records are read; a single
  non-validated record makes the layer refuse. No agent set any validation status:
  the flood and fire gates were closed by a human ([DEC-007](glossary.qmd#dec-007)).
- **One cropland convention.** Union cropland is the headline, intersection the
  conservative low bound ([DEC-015](glossary.qmd#dec-015)). Flood records (per
  event-date) are collapsed to the **peak single event-date** so persistent pixels
  are not double-counted across dates; the season-reference column is the upper
  bracket ([DEC-033](glossary.qmd#dec-033)).
- **Loss = hectares × the 2025 drought-floor yield** (~0.223 t/ha,
  [DEC-019](glossary.qmd#dec-019)). Applying the *drought-year* yield to 2026 damaged
  area makes the loss a **conservative lower bound**: 2026 was a recovery year, so
  expected yields on the lost hectares were higher.
- **Damage hectares inherit the pipeline caveats.** Flood extent is open-water
  riverine inundation (flooded vegetation and pluvial upland under-detected,
  [DEC-023](glossary.qmd#dec-023)); burned cropland is VIIRS-confirmed and a
  conservative lower bound ([DEC-031](glossary.qmd#dec-031)).
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
**Not an IPC classification.** The indicative pressure is a qualitative band from
loss-as-%-of-baseline, **not** an IPC phase (which needs the full protocol:
consumption, livelihoods, nutrition, mortality). GIEWS, FEWS NET and IPC are the
authoritative sources; this layer is a production-shock signal that *feeds* such an
assessment. Syria's 2025 baseline was already a record-drought, Crisis-level context,
so the 2026 shock is **incremental** pressure on top of that.
:::

{{< include _footer.qmd >}}
